title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted file uploads are definitely something we want to get to the bottom of quickly.

To start narrowing this down: **Was PDF upload working correctly before, and if so, when did you first notice the corruption?** Also, is this happening with *every* PDF you upload, or only certain ones (e.g., larger files, scanned documents, specific sources)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It was working fine before, started maybe a week ago? And honestly I don't know, it feels like every PDF is broken. Actually... now that you mention it, I think some of the smaller ones might have been okay? But I'm not sure, I'd have to check. All I know is our team is uploading contracts and reports and they're coming through damaged. We need this fixed ASAP, our customers are waiting on these documents.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for that detail — knowing it started about a week ago and that smaller files might be okay is really helpful.

Could you do a quick test for me? Take one of the corrupted PDFs and compare its file size to the original before upload. Specifically:

1. **Is the downloaded file significantly smaller than the original?** (This would suggest the file is being truncated during upload or storage.)
2. **Is it roughly the same size but still won't open?** (This would point toward an encoding or content-type issue.)

Also — are you uploading these through the web UI, the API, or a mobile app?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Look, I don't have time to sit here comparing file sizes byte by byte. I just tried downloading one of the broken ones and it looks like it's about the same size, maybe? It's not like half the file is missing or anything, it just won't open. The PDF viewer says it's damaged or not a valid PDF. We upload through the web UI. Can you guys just fix this? We've got clients breathing down our necks.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Totally understand the urgency — we want to get this resolved for you.

One quick question that will really help us zero in on the cause: **Do you upload any other file types besides PDFs (like Word docs, images, or spreadsheets)?** If so, are those also coming through damaged, or is it only PDFs?

This tells us whether the issue is in our general file upload pipeline or something specific to how we handle PDFs — which points our developers at very different parts of the code.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Ugh, I mostly deal with PDFs but yeah we upload some images and spreadsheets too. Now that you mention it, I think one of the girls on my team said something about an image looking weird after downloading it, but I didn't really pay attention because the PDFs are the main problem. Can you please just look into the upload system? Something clearly broke recently.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Binary file corruption on upload via web UI — regression from ~1 week ago","problem":"Files uploaded through the web UI (confirmed for PDFs, likely also images) are silently corrupted. Downloaded files are approximately the same size as the originals but cannot be opened — PDF viewers report the file as damaged/invalid. Multiple customers are affected. Smaller files may be unaffected, suggesting the corruption may be size-dependent or that the reporter hasn't tested small files carefully.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A recent change (~1 week ago) to the upload pipeline is corrupting binary data in transit or at rest. The file size being preserved rules out truncation and points to a content transformation issue. Most likely candidates: (1) a middleware or proxy change that re-encodes the request body (e.g., treating binary as UTF-8 text, which destroys non-ASCII bytes while preserving approximate size), (2) a double base64 encoding/decoding mismatch, or (3) a compression layer that corrupts the stream. The fact that multiple binary formats are affected confirms this is in the general upload path, not PDF-specific processing.","reproduction_steps":["Upload a PDF file (>100KB to be safe) to a task via the web UI","Download the same file from the task","Attempt to open the downloaded file — it will be reported as damaged","Compare the uploaded and downloaded file sizes (should be approximately equal)","Optionally compare binary content with a hex diff to identify the corruption pattern"],"environment":"Web UI upload path. No specific browser/OS dependency reported (likely server-side). Production environment, multiple customers affected.","severity":"high","impact":"Multiple customers cannot use the file attachment feature for PDFs or potentially any binary file type. Customers using TaskFlow for document exchange with their own clients are directly blocked. No known workaround.","recommended_fix":"1. Review all deployments and config changes to the upload pipeline from the past 1-2 weeks. 2. Binary-diff a corrupted download against its original to identify the corruption pattern (UTF-8 replacement characters, double-encoding artifacts, etc.). 3. Check for new middleware, proxy config changes, or library upgrades in the upload path. 4. Pay special attention to any Content-Type or Transfer-Encoding handling changes, multipart form parsing updates, or blob storage client library upgrades.","proposed_test_case":"Upload a known-good PDF (with a precomputed SHA-256 hash) via the web UI, download it, and assert the hash matches. Repeat for images and spreadsheets. Add this as a regression test in the upload integration test suite.","information_gaps":["Exact corruption pattern (hex diff between original and downloaded file) — developer can determine this","Whether the corruption happens during upload, storage, or download — developer can test each stage","Whether smaller files are truly unaffected or the reporter just hasn't verified — developer can test with various sizes","Specific deployment or config change that introduced the regression — check deploy history"]}
